statement: Crowning occurs when the baby's head is visible at the vaginal opening and does not retract between contractions.
rationale: Crowning indicates delivery is imminent. The head remains visible even between contractions. Do not transport when crowning is present. Prepare for delivery on scene. Attempting transport with crowning baby risks delivery in the ambulance.

statement: During delivery the head should be supported but not pulled as it emerges.
rationale: Support the baby's head as it delivers but do not pull. Pulling can cause injury to the baby or mother. The baby will deliver with contractions. Gentle support and guidance is appropriate but let the natural process occur.

statement: After the head delivers check for the umbilical cord wrapped around the neck.
rationale: Nuchal cord occurs in about 25% of deliveries. After head delivers run fingers along the neck to check for cord. If present gently slip it over the head or clamp and cut if too tight. This prevents strangulation during delivery.

statement: The baby's head typically delivers face down then rotates to the side for shoulder delivery.
rationale: Normal vertex delivery has the head deliver face down occiput anterior then rotate externally to align with the shoulders. Support this rotation gently. The shoulders then deliver with gentle downward then upward guidance.

statement: After delivery the baby should be dried stimulated and kept warm.
rationale: Newborns lose heat rapidly. Immediately dry the baby thoroughly which also stimulates breathing. Replace wet blankets with dry ones. Keep the baby warm ideally skin-to-skin with the mother. Hypothermia is dangerous for newborns.

statement: The umbilical cord should be clamped and cut within seconds of delivery.
rationale: Delayed cord clamping for 1-3 minutes is now recommended for healthy newborns as it improves iron stores. In an emergency with a vigorous baby there is no urgency to cut the cord immediately. Clamp in two places and cut between.

statement: A newborn's APGAR score is assessed at 1 minute and 5 minutes after birth.
rationale: APGAR assesses Appearance color Pulse heart rate Grimace reflex irritability Activity muscle tone and Respirations at 1 and 5 minutes. Scores range from 0-10 with higher scores indicating better condition. Low scores indicate need for intervention.

statement: Vigorous crying in a newborn indicates adequate respiratory effort.
rationale: A crying newborn is breathing. Crying requires air movement and indicates the baby has transitioned to breathing air. Absence of crying or weak cry may indicate respiratory compromise requiring stimulation and possibly assisted ventilation.

statement: Newborns who are not breathing or have heart rate below 60 need immediate CPR.
rationale: Newborn resuscitation begins with warming drying and stimulation. If no response provide positive pressure ventilation. If heart rate remains below 60 despite effective ventilation begin chest compressions. Newborn CPR ratio is 3:1 compressions to breaths.

statement: The placenta typically delivers within 30 minutes after the baby.
rationale: The third stage of labor is placental delivery usually occurring 5-30 minutes after birth. Do not pull on the cord. Allow natural delivery. Save the placenta for hospital examination. Bleeding may occur if placenta is incomplete.

statement: Postpartum hemorrhage is defined as blood loss greater than 500 mL after vaginal delivery.
rationale: Some bleeding after delivery is normal but excessive bleeding more than 500 mL is postpartum hemorrhage. Causes include uterine atony retained placenta and lacerations. Uterine massage oxygen and rapid transport are indicated.

statement: Massaging the uterus after delivery helps it contract and reduces bleeding.
rationale: Uterine atony failure to contract is the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage. Fundal massage stimulates contraction. Locate the fundus at the umbilicus and massage firmly. Having the baby breastfeed also stimulates uterine contraction.

statement: Breech presentation with buttocks or feet presenting first increases risk of complications.
rationale: Breech deliveries have higher risk of cord prolapse and head entrapment. If a foot or buttocks presents support the presenting part do not pull and transport immediately if possible. Hospital delivery with possible cesarean is preferred.

statement: Prolapsed umbilical cord is an emergency requiring immediate intervention.
rationale: When the cord delivers before the baby it can be compressed cutting off fetal blood supply. Insert gloved hand and lift the presenting part off the cord. Keep pressure off the cord keep it moist and transport emergently for cesarean delivery.

statement: Shoulder dystocia occurs when the baby's shoulders get stuck after the head delivers.
rationale: Shoulder dystocia is an emergency where the anterior shoulder is stuck behind the pubic bone. The baby cannot be delivered and time is critical due to cord compression. McRoberts maneuver flexing mother's thighs sharply back often helps.

statement: Meconium-stained amniotic fluid can indicate fetal distress.
rationale: Meconium is fetal stool passed in utero sometimes due to stress. Green or brown amniotic fluid indicates meconium. If present be prepared to suction the baby's airway before stimulation. Meconium aspiration can cause severe respiratory problems.

statement: Premature babies are at higher risk for hypothermia and respiratory problems.
rationale: Premature infants have immature lungs lacking surfactant and less body fat for thermoregulation. They need extra attention to warming and may require respiratory support. Handle gently as premature infants are fragile.

statement: Multiple births twins or more have higher rates of complications.
rationale: Multiple gestations have increased risk of preterm delivery malpresentation cord entanglement and postpartum hemorrhage due to overdistended uterus. Be prepared for each baby to need separate resuscitation equipment.

statement: Most newborns require only warmth drying and stimulation to begin breathing.
rationale: About 90% of newborns transition successfully with basic care. Drying provides stimulation and prevents heat loss. Most will breathe within 30 seconds. Only about 10% require more intervention and fewer than 1% need extensive resuscitation.

statement: The newborn CPR compression to ventilation ratio is 3:1 different from adults.
rationale: Newborn resuscitation uses 3:1 ratio because most newborn arrests are respiratory in origin. Ventilation is critical. Compress one-third chest depth at a rate allowing 90 compressions and 30 breaths per minute.

statement: Newborn compressions are performed using the two-thumb encircling hands technique.
rationale: For newborn CPR place both thumbs on the lower sternum with fingers encircling the chest. This provides good compression depth and control. An alternative is two-finger technique if alone and needing to access the umbilicus.

statement: Blow-by oxygen or oxygen mask provides adequate respiratory support for most distressed newborns.
rationale: Free-flow oxygen does not provide positive pressure ventilation needed for apneic or poorly breathing newborns. Positive pressure ventilation with bag-mask is required for newborns who are not breathing or have heart rate below 100 despite stimulation.

statement: Patients with disabilities should be treated with dignity and respect like all patients.
rationale: People with disabilities deserve respectful care. Address the patient directly not just caregivers. Ask about their needs and communication preferences. Do not make assumptions about their abilities or limitations.

statement: Service animals may accompany patients and should be transported with them when possible.
rationale: Service animals provide essential support for people with disabilities. Make reasonable efforts to transport the animal with the patient. If not possible ensure the animal is secured and arrangements made for its care.

statement: When communicating with a deaf patient writing notes or using a communication app can help.
rationale: Written communication visual aids and communication apps can facilitate interaction with deaf patients. Face the patient when speaking for lip reading. Learn basic signs if possible. Be patient and ensure understanding of important information.

statement: Patients with developmental disabilities may have difficulty communicating symptoms accurately.
rationale: Some patients may have difficulty describing symptoms or understanding questions. Use simple language be patient and observe non-verbal cues. Caregivers can provide valuable baseline information. Do not dismiss symptoms because communication is difficult.

statement: Obese patients may require additional resources for safe lifting and transport.
rationale: Bariatric patients may exceed weight limits for standard equipment. Special stretchers lifting devices and additional personnel may be needed. Plan ahead for safe patient handling to prevent injury to patient and providers.

statement: Standard blood pressure cuffs may give inaccurate readings on obese patients.
rationale: A cuff that is too small for arm circumference gives falsely high readings. Use appropriate sized large adult or thigh cuffs for accurate measurement. Note if proper cuff was not available and readings may be inaccurate.

statement: Obese patients may desaturate more quickly due to reduced respiratory reserve.
rationale: Obesity decreases functional residual capacity and respiratory compliance. Obese patients have less oxygen reserve and desaturate faster during apnea or hypoventilation. Position sitting up when possible and be prepared for rapid airway intervention.

statement: CPR in obese patients may require modifications for effective compressions.
rationale: Achieving adequate compression depth may be difficult due to body habitus. Position patient on firm surface use stool for leverage if needed and rotate compressors frequently as fatigue occurs faster. Standard hand position applies.

statement: Patients with tracheostomies breathe through the stoma not through nose and mouth.
rationale: A tracheostomy is a surgical airway opening in the neck. Patients breathe through this stoma. Some have total laryngectomies and cannot breathe through upper airway at all. Airway management and ventilation must be through the stoma.

statement: Patients with feeding tubes are at risk for aspiration.
rationale: Feeding tubes deliver nutrition directly to stomach or intestine but do not prevent aspiration of oral secretions. Patients with feeding tubes often have swallowing dysfunction. Position upright when possible and suction as needed.

statement: Patients on home ventilators need their ventilator or BVM support during transport.
rationale: Ventilator-dependent patients cannot breathe adequately without support. Bring the home ventilator if possible or provide BVM ventilation during transport. Know the patient's ventilator settings and ensure adequate oxygen supply.

statement: Central venous catheters can be used for emergency medication or fluid administration.
rationale: Central lines provide vascular access that can be used in emergencies. However use requires training and sterile technique. If trained and protocols allow these can be used for medications. Never inject air into central lines.

statement: Patients with pacemakers or implanted defibrillators can still have cardiac emergencies.
rationale: Pacemakers and ICDs do not prevent all cardiac emergencies. The device may malfunction be overwhelmed by severe arrhythmia or the patient may have other cardiac problems. Treat the patient based on presentation not just the device.

statement: Hospice patients have chosen to focus on comfort care rather than curative treatment.
rationale: Hospice care emphasizes quality of life and comfort for patients with terminal illnesses who have chosen to forego aggressive life-prolonging treatments. Respect these wishes while providing appropriate symptom management.

statement: A valid DNR order should be honored even for hospice patients experiencing distress.
rationale: DNR orders remain valid. Hospice patients may still experience symptoms requiring treatment and transport. Honor DNR status but provide appropriate comfort care and symptom management. Discuss wishes with patient family and hospice staff.

statement: Hospice patients may still require emergency medical services for symptom management or caregiver support.
rationale: EMS may be called for hospice patients experiencing uncontrolled pain respiratory distress or other symptoms. The goal is symptom relief not resuscitation. Family may also need support. Work with hospice team when possible.

statement: Mental health emergencies require the same professional approach as medical emergencies.
rationale: Psychiatric patients deserve respectful professional care. Maintain calm demeanor ensure safety for everyone and transport for evaluation. Mental health conditions are medical conditions. Stigma should not affect quality of care.

statement: Patients experiencing psychosis may be responding to hallucinations or delusions.
rationale: Psychotic patients may hear voices see things others do not or hold fixed false beliefs. They are responding to their perceived reality. Do not argue with delusions. Ensure safety speak calmly and transport for psychiatric evaluation.

statement: Physical restraint should only be used as a last resort after verbal de-escalation fails.
rationale: Verbal de-escalation is the first approach for agitated patients. Physical restraint carries risks including positional asphyxia and should only be used when necessary for safety. If used monitor continuously and document thoroughly.

statement: Suicidal patients should be taken seriously and transported for evaluation.
rationale: All suicidal statements or behaviors should be taken seriously. Patients need psychiatric evaluation to assess risk and provide intervention. Remove access to means of harm when possible. Do not leave suicidal patients alone.

statement: Cultural beliefs may affect how patients perceive illness and interact with healthcare providers.
rationale: Culture influences health beliefs treatment preferences communication styles and family involvement in care. Be aware of and respect cultural differences while providing appropriate medical care. Ask about preferences when possible.

statement: Language barriers can significantly impact patient assessment and care.
rationale: Communication is essential for history-taking obtaining consent and providing instructions. Use professional interpreters when available. Speak clearly use simple terms and confirm understanding. Family members may help but may not interpret accurately.

statement: Religious beliefs may affect patient decisions about treatment including blood transfusions and end-of-life care.
rationale: Some religions have specific beliefs about medical treatments. Respect patient autonomy regarding their beliefs while providing information about medical consequences. Document patient wishes and reasons for refusing specific treatments.

statement: Homeless patients may have delayed access to healthcare resulting in more advanced disease.
rationale: Lack of regular healthcare access means conditions may be more advanced when homeless patients seek emergency care. They may also have untreated chronic conditions and limited ability to follow up. Provide thorough assessment and appropriate referrals.

statement: Homeless patients deserve the same respectful care as all other patients.
rationale: Homelessness does not diminish a person's dignity or right to quality care. Treat homeless patients with the same respect and thoroughness as any patient. Personal biases should not affect professional care delivery.

statement: Environmental exposure is a significant risk for homeless patients.
rationale: Without adequate shelter homeless individuals are at risk for hypothermia hyperthermia and weather-related injuries. Assess for environmental exposure even when the chief complaint is different. Address shelter needs when possible.

statement: Healthcare providers as patients may minimize symptoms or resist care.
rationale: Healthcare workers may downplay symptoms self-diagnose or feel uncomfortable as patients. Treat them professionally assess thoroughly and do not let their medical knowledge influence your clinical judgment about their condition.

statement: EMS personnel responding to calls involving colleagues should maintain professional standards.
rationale: Responding to emergencies involving colleagues or family members is emotionally challenging. Maintain professionalism provide standard care and request additional resources if you cannot remain objective. Quality care must not be compromised.
